data.title: NAHB Housing Market Index: Builder Confidence Edges Lower
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Builder confidence edged lower for a second straight month in February as persistent affordability challenges continue to dampen builder sentiment. The National Association of Home Builders (NAHB) Housing Market Index (HMI) dropped one point from January to 36 this month, marking the 22nd consecutive negative reading. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 38.

The National Association of Home Builders Housing Market Index is a gauge of builder opinion on the relative level of current and future single-family home sales.

The data is collected from a monthly survey of about 900 home builders asking respondents to, "rate market conditions for the sale of new homes at the present time and in the next six months as well as the traffic of prospective buyers of new homes." It is a diffusion index, which means that a reading above 50 indicates a favorable outlook on home sales; below 50 indicates a negative outlook.

Here are a few quotes from the press release:

“Builders reduced their expectations for future sales as buyers report affordability challenges, which is contributing to declining consumer confidence for the overall economy,” said NAHB Chairman Buddy Hughes, a home builder and developer from Lexington, N.C. “While the majority of builders continue to deploy buyer incentives, including price cuts, many prospective buyers remain on the sidelines. Although demand for new construction has weakened, remodeling demand has remained solid given a lack of household mobility.”

“Housing affordability remains an ongoing challenge at the start of 2026,” said NAHB Chief Economist Robert Dietz. “The solution for the housing market is the enactment of policies that will bend the construction cost curve and enable additional supply of attainable housing. On the positive side, easing inflation should continue to allow lower interest rates for mortgages and builder loans.”

Here is the historical series, which dates from 1985.

Components of the NAHB Housing Market Index

The NAHB Housing Market Index is calculated based off of three components: current sales, expected sales in the next six months, and traffic of prospective buyers. In February, current sales were unchanged while expected sales and buyer traffic moved lower.

Housing Market Index: Regional Breakdown

The chart below gives a regional breakdown of the NAHB HMI in four regions of the country: Northeast, Midwest, South, and West.

Housing Market Index and Consumer Attitudes

The HMI correlates fairly closely with broad measures of consumer confidence. Here is a pair of overlays with the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index.

ETFs associated with home builders include Invesco Dynamic Building & Construction ETF (PKB), iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF (ITB) and SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: U.S. Dollar Index Tries To Break 2026 Downtrend Despite 14-Year Record Bear Positioning
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The US dollar is at the center of traders' attention, and it has been the case ever since Trump was nominated for a second term. Ever since the beginning of 2026, the greenback has seen quite a downturn, driven by narratives of a new world order, one in which the US dollar wouldn't play a similar role as market stabilizer.

Exacerbated by the Greenland crisis and Trump's repeated attempts to devalue the USD, the DXY reached 5-year lows just ahead of the end-of-January FOMC meeting.

Things changed quickly right after Kevin Warsh was nominated to be the next Fed Chair. While the next head of the Federal Reserve is seen as a reformist, relative hawk, he still hasn't said a word, sparking a round of heavy speculation among participants.

An interesting graph regarding his bias was shared on Financial-Net, but some argued that the shifts in his dovishness and hawkishness were more closely tied to actual economic activity. It still leaves room for doubts regarding his tone.

He will still have to be officially nominated by the Senate before his term is confirmed, as senators like Thom Tillis won't back the vote while Jerome Powell's investigation is still ongoing – it could indeed set a dangerous precedent for Federal Reserve independence.

Nevertheless, with all the bearishness still waning on the US dollar, it is difficult not to take a contrarian view. Positioning against the greenback is the most pessimistic in 14 years, and despite the aggressive views, the DXY remains in an 8-month range (between ~96.00 and 100.00).

If data like the recent non-farm payrolls surprise to the upside once again, a short squeeze in the dollar isn't such an impossible scenario, particularly with the reserve currency not weakening the slightest after Friday's soft CPI.

Don't forget that Friday should see the SCOTUS announcement on the legality of Trump's tariffs. Reactions can be very mixed, but when looking at the reactions last May to them almost getting blocked, their ban could see an upwards reaction.

We’ll dive into an in-depth technical analysis of DXY to examine the current technical outlook for the US dollar after last week's huge data and ahead of Friday's Core PCE report.

Dollar Index (DXY) Multi-Timeframe Analysis

Daily Chart

The dollar index has bounced meaningfully from its end-January troughs and is now attempting to break out of its early 2026 downward channel. Rebounding this morning on its 97.50 mid-range pivot area, bears keep a slight advantage, but looking further out, it doesn't look like any side is under control.

Contrarian views to the current positioning and absence of reactions to negative dollar headlines point to a bearish stance that could be overstretched. Let's take a closer look.

4H Chart and Technical Levels

Today's trading was very erratic in the US dollar, seeing a huge bounce throughout the morning session before giving its lead back, now close to unchanged on the day.

Nevertheless, the index is now back above its 50-period moving average, pointing to bulls taking the short-term advantage and the action resembling a breakout-retest formation, prompting further continuation.

A close above 97.60 will be required to confirm further upside.

Levels to place on your DXY charts:

Resistance Levels

  • August and mini-range pivot 97.25 to 97.60
  • Mini resistance 98.80 to 99.00
  • 99.40 to 99.50 January tesistance
  • 100.376 November highs

Support Levels

  • 4H 50-period MA 97.08
  • 2025 Lows: major support 96.50 to 97.00 (mini-range lows, 4H 50-MA)
  • Early 2022 consolidation just below 96.00
  • Trump USD flash crash: 95.55
  • 95.00 main psychological support

1H Chart

Turning to the intraday timeframe, the chart once again looks quite mixed. The price action really points to a break-retest scenario, but the way the dollar got rejected after its overnight/morning rally gives more confusing vibes.

To get more clarity, pay close attention to these levels:

  • The 97.00 key level should act as strong support, particularly as it coincides with the 4H 50-MA and the counter-trend underpin.
  • Any candle close below on high momentum would warrant further downside in the dollar
  • An imminent rebound would require a break above the 97.55 session highs and could then be headed to a test of the early January highs (99.495).
    • Look for FX pairs that offer interesting setups to profit from these scenarios – GBP/USD, USD/CHF and NZD/USD could be interesting in those aspects (watch out for the RBNZ meeting for the latter).

Safe trades!

Original Post

data.title: BIBL: Biblical Values-Focused Strategy Outperforming In 2026 Has Disadvantages, A Hold
data.description:
In my view, the Inspire 100 ETF (BIBL) represents an interesting, adequately liquid vehicle with a high-quality portfolio that investors seeking a biblical values-informed investment strategy should add to their watchlist.

However, I see no argument compelling enough on why I should initiate coverage of this name with a Buy rating. BIBL has been doing excellently this year owing to its tilt toward cyclical names (which is more of a by-product and not a direct consequence of its strategy), yet its performance tracing back to 2017 has been far from excellent, as it has meaningfully underperformed the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) while also not offering anything particularly impressive on the risk front. Besides, while I like its factor mix, I find the one of the S&P 500 ETF way superior. So, all these considerations led me to the decision to assign a Hold rating to BIBL.

BIBL Strategy And Portfolio

In essence, as explained in the summary prospectus available on its website, BIBL

... seeks to replicate investment results that generally correspond, before fees and expenses, to the performance of the Inspire 100 Index (the “100 Index”).

The lynchpin of the index is the "Inspire Impact Score™":

The Fund generally invests at least 80% of its total assets in the component securities of the 100 Index. Inspire Investing, LLC (the “Adviser” or “Index Provider”), the Fund’s index provider (and also the Fund’s investment adviser) selects domestic large capitalization equity securities and which have an Inspire Impact Score™ of zero or higher. The Adviser defines large capitalization companies to be those that are the largest 10% of the U.S. equity market.

The following description of the score is offered:

The Inspire Impact Score™ is a proprietary selection methodology that is designed to assign a score to a particular security based on the security’s alignment with biblical values and the positive impact the issuing company has on the world.

There are multiple "exclusionary categories" that could earn a company a negative score, including alcohol, gambling, tobacco, etc. I should mention that there is also a rather extensive list of "biblical alignment categories" that, on the contrary, secure a positive score, including air quality, customer privacy, data security, ethical sales practices, etc. At the end of the day, the idea is to include 100 names:

The 100 securities without a negative Inspire Impact Scores™ are included in the 100 Index and are market capitalization weighted so that components with higher market values have a higher weighting in the 100 Index.

BIBL vs. IVV: Portfolio Composition

How is BIBL different from the S&P 500 ETF? As of February 13, there were 100 equities in its portfolio (plus the BBH Sweep Vehicle accounting for 8 bps), with the key name being Caterpillar Inc. (CAT), a heavyweight name in the construction machinery & heavy transportation equipment industry that hardly needs a wordy introduction, with an 8.95% weight (vs. just 0.61% in IVV). Overall, the industrials sector is BIBL's essential allocation (with about 19.9% more weight than it has in the IVV portfolio), accounting for close to 29%, also responsible for the significant share of its YTD gains (my analysis shows a 10.61% median price return for this group of 23 stocks).

Information technology is the second-largest allocation, with 26 holdings and a 26.82% weight, which is considerably lower than IVV's 32.9% allocation. On a side note, IVV's exposure to IT has decreased from 34.4% as of January 1 to the current level owing to the 2026 tech rout.

Other differences I believe investors should pay attention to are that BIBL is underweight in consumer discretionary, health care, consumer staples, and financials. Importantly, BIBL entirely ignores the communication sector (this fact also played a role in its YTD outperformance, as out of 23 communication stocks in the IVV portfolio, 17 have delivered negative price returns this year).

Factor Exposures

Despite having an 88.7% overlap with IVV, BIBL has a factor mix that is considerably different in a few ways: light in growth (no exposure to tech bellwethers, and the entire communication sector is in part to blame), with slightly (not critically, of course) lower exposure to quality. Uncoincidentally, considering the capital rotation, the Inspire 100 ETF has larger exposure to momentum stocks (those that earned a B- Quant Momentum grade or higher).

My calculations show that, in part, these differences can be explained by the fact that out of 75 S&P 500 constituents that have qualified for its portfolio, BIBL is overweight in 71 names. Also, BIBL ignores the trillion-dollar league completely, with the largest company in the mix being the above-mentioned CAT, which had a market cap of $362.3 billion as of February 15.

BIBL Portfolio Valuation Nuances

What is interesting, though, is that despite having a weighted average market cap of only $103.6 billion, BIBL does not have a decisive edge over IVV in terms of value. It is true that it has a slightly larger exposure to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade or higher and a lower weighted average Price/Sales ratio, but the adjusted WA earnings yield and EV/EBITDA tell a different story.

I found this issue worthy of further research, with my initial hypothesis being that, owing to the capital rotation, some names in the BIBL portfolio have seen multiple expansion this year. To assess that, I used the financial metrics dataset I built for the analysis of the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap ETF™ (SCHX) (alas, I did not track the BIBL portfolio in December). The financial data I used was as of December 30.

  • My calculations show that since December 30, 57 names (out of 95 BIBL's holdings that were present in the SCHX portfolio as well) have seen EV/EBITDA expansion, with the median increase being 9.3%.
  • All the stocks in this group, except for Molina Healthcare, Inc. (MOH), have delivered gains in 2026. Moreover, each name in this cohort has outperformed IVV (which is down by 3 bps YTD as of writing this article).

So, these facts at least partly explain why the BIBL portfolio looks a bit expensive.

BIBL Is Not A GARP Play

Also, in terms of GARP characteristics, the BIBL portfolio is considerably behind that of IVV, especially when it comes to the PEG ratio (the weighted average P/E ratio divided by the forward EPS growth rate).

Metric BIBL IVV
PEG Ratio 2.195 1.238
Price/Sales-to-Growth Ratio 0.665 0.536
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 1.785 1.038

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

BIBL Performance

Positives

It is quite obvious that one of BIBL's main advantages performance-wise is its YTD returns.

Next, my calculations show that over the November 2017–January 2026 period, BIBL outperformed IVV in 52 calendar months (out of the total of 99), which is a good result, especially considering the median active monthly return was at 7 bps.

Negatives

However, there are more negatives. For example, BIBL has just one full calendar year of outperformance vs. IVV, namely 2020.

Year BIBL Active Return (IVV used as a benchmark)
November–December 2017 0.01%
2018 -3.06%
2019 -1.57%
2020 4.24%
2021 -1.03%
2022 -5.10%
2023 -8.45%
2024 -12.44%
2025 -0.58%
January 2026 4.42%

Calculated by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

Next, over November 2017–January 2026, BIBL underperformed IVV by 3% in annualized return, delivering a higher standard deviation, weaker risk-adjusted returns (measured using Sharpe and Sortino ratios), as well as a downside capture ratio of 114.41%.

An argument can be made, though, that the market regime is quietly (or not so quietly, depending on the perspective) changing and that the current capital rotation opens the door for a prolonged period of BIBL's outperformance. This is a clearly nice assumption, but I see one problem here: in the 2020s, there has already been a capital rotation to cyclicals in 2021, yet BIBL underperformed IVV that year.

Final Thoughts

In summary, my opinion is that BIBL could be a good choice for investors seeking not only to gain exposure to "biblically aligned companies" but also who would appreciate a vehicle that ignores tech bellwethers, favoring cheaper cyclicals without a significant decrease in quality characteristics. For them, BIBL could qualify for a shortlist of possible satellite holdings. However, it is worth remembering that its expense ratio is 35 bps vs. IVV's 3 bps.

However, it is worth remembering that this is not a strategy that targets sectors or certain style factors. Roughly speaking, it targets values, offering exposure to "the most inspiring, biblically aligned U.S. large cap companies," and its factor characteristics and sector allocations are a by-product of this.

What might contribute to the rating change in the future?

While I assign the Hold rating to BIBL, the signs of the capital rotation broadening and deepening might prompt me to reconsider this decision and become more optimistic about BIBL.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Index Outlook: Traders Are Looking For Direction
data.description:
By Elior Manier

US traders just came back from a long weekend break after yesterday's President's Day celebrations. Last week concluded a bizarre week, with a very positive CPI report not materializing into a full-on rebound for equities.

As a matter of fact, the Dow Jones broke back below 50,000; tech stocks are still under heavy pressure, and overall, stock markets have been directionless for the past three months.

Directionless doesn't mean a market top, but what stands out from recent weeks' action is that investors will really need a concrete shift in sentiment before indexes can start heading to new highs again.

A recent Bank of America survey still points to heavy positioning and low cash holdings among asset managers (implying that the bullet clips are close to empty). Yet, indexes remain well within 10% of their all-time highs and have resisted the large waves of outflows in the AI/tech sectors.

With the current session forming daily dojis and overall trading unchanged across all US indexes (except Nasdaq, down a modest 0.30%), the current signal is one of confusion.

US rates are not expected to change until at least the May meeting. Participants are still awaiting Kevin Warsh's views, and the tariff decision (expected to be rejected by the Supreme Court) could significantly affect the current course of action.

Holding well despite uncertainty is a sign of relative strength, but it can quickly be overturned by worsening news. Keep a very close eye on new data from the US; geopolitics seems to be stabilizing, but remains a volatility factor.

This screams for range-bound action until we learn more.

Dive into today’s session charts and key trading levels for the major US indices: the Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The Dow has now been holding within a tight range for the past 4 sessions, which provides traders with clear breakout boundaries.

  • A break and close above the 4H 50-period moving average (49,775) would confirm a return above 50,000 and should relaunch prospects for continued all-time highs
  • A break and close below the 200 MA (49,257), however, would lead to a retest of the broader range lows around 48,000.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • January highs: key pivot 49,500 to 49,700 (4H 50 MA)
  • Intraday resistance: 50,250 (rejecting)
  • ATH resistance 50,400 to 50,500
  • Index all-time highs: 50,512

Support Levels

  • 4H 200 MA 49,250
  • Major support – 49,000
  • Past week support 48,600 to 48,700
  • Key support around 47,500
  • 45,000 psychological level (main support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

It is difficult to remain as bearish on the Nasdaq when it really remains so resilient. Now forming the basis of a double bottom at its key support, and the RSI turning higher, it seems that bears are now getting quite exhausted.

The path of least resistance now seems to be at least for a retest of the pivot zone at 25,000 and could extend to its 4H 200-MA (25,400). Bears will want to see a clean break and close below 24,000.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • 25,400 to 25,500 key intraday resistance (2H 200 MA)
  • Pivotal resistance 25,700 to 25,850
  • 26,246 FOMC highs
  • All-time high resistance zone 26,100 to 26,300

Support Levels

  • 24,500 to 25,600 key support (current rebound)
  • February 5 lows: 24,165
  • October-November support: 23,800 to 24,000
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 support

S&P 500 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 has been holding one of the cleanest ranges seen in ages in equity benchmarks and is currently standing at its lows, prompting similar views as in the Nasdaq. (I hope some traders caught our early call for range-bound conditions.)

A test of its 50 and 200 moving averages (around 6,920) will come as a final line for bears to turn the consolidation into something materially subject to downward continuation; if not, expect the 6,750 to 7,000 range to hold until traders receive more news.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Previous ATH resistance 6,945 to 6,975 (rejecting)
  • Session top 6,996
  • Current ATH 7,020
  • All-time high resistance: 7,000 to 7,020 (range highs)

Support Levels

  • 6,920 session lows and 2H MA 50/200 (key barometer)
  • Pivotal support zone 6,880 to 6,900
  • Mini support 6,830 to 6,850
  • 6,800 psychological support
  • Overnight lows 6,735 (range lows)
  • 6,400 major psychological support

Safe trades, and keep a close eye on the US-Iran developments!

Original Post

data.title: LightPath Technologies: Still Growing, But Valuation And Technical Concerns Loom
data.description:
I wrote about the growth potential for LightPath Technologies (LPTH) back in 2018. The stock increased 473% since then, while the S&P 500 (SPY) gained 135%. LightPath just reported earnings for Q2 FY26, which provides insights into the company's future growth.

LightPath is growing at a strong pace, but the stock is overvalued, while the technical outlook indicates a possible near-term pullback.

Insights From LightPath's Q2 FY26 Earnings Report

LightPath reported mixed results for Q2 FY26. Revenue increased 122% yoy to $16.4 million, beating analysts' estimates by $1.44 million. GAAP EPS decreased 186% to a loss of ($0.20) over the loss of ($0.07) from Q2 FY25. The GAAP EPS missed estimates by $0.16.

The revenue gains were largely driven by a 7x yoy increase in assemblies and modules sales to $7.2 million. LightPath's revenue gains were also driven by a 21% gain in visible components to $3.4 million and a 61% gain in infrared component sales to $5 million. Revenue from engineering services remained flat at $0.7 million.

The good news on the earnings side is that LPTH had a strong gain in the gross margin from 26% in Q2 FY25 to 37% in Q2 FY26. Continued revenue and margin gains will help LightPath to achieve profitability in the future. The increase in the gross margin was mostly attributed to the increase in assemblies and modules sales, which is a higher-margin business.

LightPath's expenses are what is currently keeping the company from turning a profit. The company had total operating expenses of $7.1 million in Q2, which was higher than the gross profit of $6 million. The company is getting closer to profitability - it just may take another one to two years to reach it. Continued revenue growth and gross margin increases can get the company to profitability.

LightPath's CEO, Sam Rubin, explained how the company is transitioning to be vertically integrated. Here's what he stated: "We entered calendar year 2026 having completed the first part of our transition to a vertically integrated provider of high-value infrared optics and camera systems, geared towards driving higher revenue and gross margins. The 2nd quarter demonstrates this transition with measurable commercial success, record revenue, and margin improvement."

By becoming vertically integrated, LightPath can provide a more complete value to its customers. The company now provides camera systems in addition to its precision optical components. This is a transformation from components to systems. This can help LightPath to obtain more lucrative contracts from defense and industrial customers over the long term.

LightPath's next phase involves rapid scaling over the next 3 years to increase market share. Unfortunately, LightPath didn't elaborate on how the company will accomplish this rapid scaling. They did state that there will be more discussions about that during LightPath's Investor Day webcast in a couple of weeks. No specific date was provided for the webcast.

As of the end of December 31, 2025, LPTH had a total backlog of $97.8 million. This is nearly a 5x increase over the backlog of $19.8 million from the end of 2024. This strong backlog growth should provide multiple years of revenue growth for LightPath.

LightPath's main offering is known as Black Diamond. This is LPTH's proprietary chalcogenide glass, which is a secure alternative to germanium for infrared imaging. Black Diamond has the company positioned well for the 2026 National Defense Authorization Act [NDAA]. This act mandates the elimination of obtaining optical glass and related products/systems from China, Russia, and other select countries no later than January 1, 2030. The optical glass from these countries typically contains germanium.

LPTH believes it is positioned well for this legislative change with its G5 Infrared acquisition. G5 Infrared produces the industry's leading long-range infrared cameras for surveillance and counter unmanned aircraft systems [UAS]. This acquisition helps make LightPath a go-to company for mission-critical defense and aerospace applications. LPTH has obtained $80 million in new orders for G5 Infrared products since acquiring the company a year ago. This was a significant gain over the $15 million in revenue that G5 Infrared had in 2024.

By combining the Black Diamond materials with G5 cameras, LPTH can provide better execution to the market with its vertically integrated supply chain. The vertical integration can help speed up the process of getting the final products into the hands of LightPath's customers. This can help the company obtain new future contracts.

The advantage of the G5 long-range cameras (miles away) is that they have lenses that are 10 inches in diameter (used in drones). Longer-range lenses, which are used in satellite cameras from space, use cameras with even larger diameters. So, with the G5 cameras, LightPath already has the ability to produce long-range cameras without germanium. At the same time, LightPath's competitors will need to find solutions for germanium alternatives.

Given this advantage, LightPath believes that it has 3 to 4 years where the company can take significant market share while positioning itself as a dominant player in this field.

Valuation

Since LightPath is not yet profitable, I'm using the price/sales ratio to value the stock. LPTH is currently trading with a trailing price/sales of 9.5 and a forward price/sales of about 10. This is more than 3x higher than the sector median price/sales of 3.2.

It could be that the market is pricing in some of LightPath's future revenue growth. It is possible that LPTH continues to be awarded an above-average valuation for its above-average revenue growth. I just think the high valuation does present a risk for the stock to pull back in the near term.

LightPath is expected to grow revenue at about 80% in FY26 to $67 million, according to analysts' consensus estimates. Then revenue is expected to grow by 25% to $83 million in FY27. This strong growth combined with the margin expansion that LightPath has been achieving has the company on track to reach profitability in FY27 or FY28. The valuation indicates that the market could be pricing in this future profitability.

Technical Perspective

LightPath's weekly chart above shows a bearish divergence. This is indicated as the stock made a higher high from October 2025 to January 2026. At the same time, the RSI made a lower high. Bearish divergences like this typically result in significant pullbacks from what I have observed.

The blue MACD line looks like it is about to cross below the red signal line. The MACD histogram bars have been decreasing in length. This indicates a decrease in stock price momentum. An official bearish crossover along with the histogram bars turning red would indicate an official change in trend from bullish to bearish. This could lead to a larger pullback.

I zoomed in to the daily chart above to show how the stock price might be failing to reach a higher high. The stock increased up to about $12.50 before dropping again this past week. This could indicate that some investors are taking profits after the strong run-up in price.

Investors should watch the price action in the coming weeks to see how the stock trends. It is possible that the recent positive earnings report continues to drive the stock higher. On the other hand, the earnings report could act as a sell-the-news event after the stock's strong run-up in price.

Risks for the Stock

Tariffs remain a risk for LightPath. Tariffs implemented by the current U.S. administration have fluctuated significantly and can change at any time. It is possible that new tariffs could be implemented that could negatively impact LightPath's business. Tariffs could reduce the amount of demand or revenue coming from the affected countries. Tariffs could also increase the cost of supplies for LightPath, which could negatively impact margins.

LightPath obtains its revenue from a small number of customers. LPTH's top 3 customers comprised 23% of total revenue in FY25. The loss of business from one or more of these customers would have a significant negative impact on LightPath's revenue.

The company also faces geopolitical risks. For example, the conflict between Russia and Ukraine could disrupt LightPath's supply chain. I will point out that LightPath doesn't have any direct operations in Russia or Ukraine. However, LPTH could be negatively impacted by global supply disruptions as a result of the conflict. This could be from inflation, sanctions, higher energy costs, cyberattacks, and other supply chain disruptions.

LightPath's Long-Term Outlook

I'm impressed with LightPath's strong revenue growth. I also like how the company is competitively positioned to benefit from the 2026 National Defense Authorization Act. LightPath's revenue growth and margin expansion have the company on the path to profitability within about 2 years. I expect the stock to grow at a strong pace over the long term.

The issue that I have with LightPath is that the high valuation and bearish technical setup are a risk for the stock. The stock could experience a sharp pullback on profit-taking in the near term. For this reason, I'm lowering my rating for the stock to 'HOLD.'

data.title: Tracking Akre Capital Management Portfolio - Q4 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Akre Capital Management’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Akre’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 02/13/2026. Please visit our Tracking Akre Capital Management Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our last update for their moves during Q3 2025.

This quarter, Akre’s 13F portfolio value decreased from ~$10.03B to ~$9.12B. The number of positions decreased from 19 to 18. The largest five stakes are MasterCard, Brookfield, KKR, Visa, and Moody’s. They account for ~63% of the total portfolio value.

AUM is distributed among private funds, separately managed accounts, and the Akre Focus Fund ETF (AKRE). Since the 2009 inception through Q4 2025, the annualized returns are at 14.47% compared to 14.48% for the S&P 500 Index. The mutual fund was converted to an ETF in October. Akre coined the term "compounding machines" to describe the type of businesses he invests in. To learn more about that investing style, check out the book “100 to 1 in the Stock Market”.

Note: positions in the ETF not reported through the 13F filings include Constellation Software (CNSWF), LVMH (LVMUY), and Topicus.com (TOITF). Topicus.com is a spinoff from Constellation Software.

Stake Disposals:

Verisk Analytics (VRSK): The minutely small 0.02% remainder stake in VRSK was dropped this quarter.

Stake Increases:

Brookfield Corp (BN): BN is a ~14% of the portfolio stake established in Q3 2019 at prices between $14 and $17 and increased by ~18% next quarter at prices between $15 and $18. That was followed with another ~30% stake increase during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$21 and ~$23. There was a ~40% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$42 and ~$49. The stock currently trades at $47.79.

Note: the prices quoted above are adjusted for the Asset Management Business spinoff completed in December 2022 and the 3-for-2 stock-split in October 2025.

CoStar Group (CSGP): The ~7% CSGP stake was purchased in Q4 2019 at prices between ~$54 and ~$62 and increased by ~75% in the next quarter at prices between ~$52 and ~$74. Q2 2020 saw another ~120% stake increase at prices between ~$53 and ~$73. There was a ~25% reduction in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$54 and ~$68. Q2 2024 saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between $72.73 and $95.69. The stock currently trades well below their purchase price ranges at ~$45. The last several quarters saw only minor adjustments.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the 10-for-1 stock split in June 2022.

Fair Isaac Corp (FICO): The 5.14% of the portfolio FICO stake was established during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$1484 and ~$2218. The bulk of the position was built during the last quarter at prices between ~$1300 and ~$1905. The stock is now at ~$1345. There was a minor ~6% stake increase this quarter.

Copart Inc. (CPRT): CPRT is 3.38% of the portfolio position purchased during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$47 and ~$64. The last quarter saw a ~250% stake increase at prices between ~$44 and ~$50. That was followed by a ~85% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$38 and ~$46. The stock currently trades below their purchase price ranges at $37.49.

CCC Intelligent Solutions (CCC): The 2.16% CCC position was purchased during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$10.50 and ~$12.50. The next quarter saw a ~12% stake increase at prices between $10.55 and $11.98. That was followed by a ~37% stake increase during Q3 2024 at prices between $9.97 and $11.33. Q1 2025 also saw a ~24% stake increase at prices between $8.68 and $11.80. There was a ~11% further increase during Q2 2025 at prices between $8.14 and $9.64. The last quarter also saw a similar increase at prices between $9.05 and $10.50. This quarter saw a ~53% further increase at prices between ~$7 and ~$9.50. The stock is now well below their purchase price ranges at $4.94.

Goosehead Insurance (GSHD): The very small 0.30% stake in GSHD saw a marginal increase this quarter.

Stake Decreases:

Mastercard (MA): MA is currently the largest 13F stake at ~19% of the portfolio. It was first purchased in 2010 and has consistently increased since. Their overall cost-basis is ~$22 compared to the current price of ~$518 per share. Q1 2020 saw a ~9% stake increase while Q2 2022 saw a ~14% selling at prices between ~$309 and ~$379. That was followed by a ~40% reduction in the three quarters through Q2 2024 at prices between ~$365 and ~$489. Q1 2025 also saw a ~10% selling at prices between ~$500 and ~$582. There was a ~9% trimming during the last two quarters. They are harvesting gains.

KKR & Company (KKR): KKR is a ~11% (top three) of the portfolio position established in Q1 2018 at prices between $20 and $24.50 and doubled next quarter at prices between $19.50 and $25.50. Recent activity follows. Q4 2024 also saw a ~10% selling at prices between ~$130 and ~$163. There was a further ~14% trimming during the next quarter at prices between ~$105 and ~$170. The stock currently trades at ~$102. This quarter saw a minor ~7% trimming.

Note: KKR converted from a partnership to a corporation on July 1, 2018.

Moody's Corporation (MCO): The initial purchase of MCO happened in Q1 & Q2 2012 in the high-30s price-range. Recent activity follows: Q4 2024 saw a ~8% trimming at prices between ~$453 and ~$501. That was followed by a ~21% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$436 and ~$532. This quarter saw a ~18% further selling at prices between ~$466 and ~$522. It currently trades at ~$426 and is now at ~10% of the portfolio. They are harvesting gains.

Note: Akre’s cost-basis on MCO is ~$39.

O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY): ORLY is a large 6.72% portfolio stake first purchased in 2005. Over 800K shares were purchased at the time. The position was at 825K shares as of Q1 2017 - every year had seen adjustments but overall, the stake had remained remarkably steady over that twelve-year period – the stock returned ~10x during that time. 2017 saw a ~130% stake increase at an average cost of ~$13. Since then, there has been trimming most quarters. This quarter saw a ~22% reduction at prices between ~$90 and ~$108. The stock is now at $96.66. They are harvesting gains.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for 15-for-1 stock split in June.

Roper Technologies (ROP): ROP is a 5.39% of the portfolio position first purchased in Q2 & Q3 2014 at prices between $129 and $150. Recent activity follows. H1 2021 saw a ~12% trimming and that was followed by a ~5% trimming during Q3 2023. There was minor trimming in the seven quarters through Q2 2025. That was followed by a ~20% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$492 and ~$576. The stock is now at ~$321. There was a minor ~5% trimming this quarter.

American Tower (AMT): AMT has been in the portfolio for over two decades. It is now at 0.49% of the portfolio. Recent activity follows: the six years through Q3 2018 saw consistent buying almost every quarter. The share count increased over four times from 1.7M shares to 7.1M shares during that period. The buying happened at prices between $65 and $118. Recent activity follows. There was a ~53% selling during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$173 and ~$220. That was followed by a ~55% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$198 and ~$234. The last quarter also saw a similar selling at prices between ~$189 and ~$234. There was a ~42% further reduction this quarter at prices between ~$171 and ~$195. The stock is now at ~$192. They are harvesting gains.

Danaher Corporation (DHR): DHR is now a minutely small 0.17% of the portfolio position. The original stake was established in Q4 2013 at prices between $47 and $55 and increased by roughly five-times during Q2 2014 at prices between $52 and $59. There was a ~50% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$180 and ~$214. This quarter saw the position almost sold out at prices between ~$199 and ~$235. The stock is now at ~$213.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the Fortive spinoff in July 2017.

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B): The minutely small 0.07% stake in BRK.B was further reduced during the quarter.

Kept Steady:

Visa Inc. (V): Visa is a ~10% of the portfolio stake. It was established in Q2 2012 at a cost-basis of around $30. Q2 2013 saw a one-third increase in the low-40s and that was followed with a 60% increase in Q2 2016 in the high 70s. Q1 2017 also saw another roughly one-third increase at prices between $79.50 and $90. Q4 2023 saw a ~27% selling at prices between ~$229 and ~$262. The stock is now at ~$314. There was a ~8% trimming during the last quarter.

Note: Visa stock split 4-for-1 in March 2015. The prices quoted above are adjusted for that split.

Airbnb (ABNB): ABNB is a 5.17% of the portfolio position established during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$141 and ~$165. The next quarter saw a ~180% stake increase at prices between ~$113 and ~$154. That was followed by a ~10% further increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$116 and ~$164. There was a ~20% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$100 and ~$144. The stock currently trades at ~$121.

SOPHiA GENETICS SA (SOPH): The very small 0.19% SOPH stake saw a two-thirds increase during Q2 2025 at prices between $2.58 and $3.50. The stock currently trades at $4.74.

Note: they have a ~5.6% ownership stake in the business.

CarMax Inc. (KMX): The minutely small 0.06% remainder stake in KMX was kept steady this quarter.

Below is a spreadsheet that highlights the changes to Akre Capital Management’s 13F stock portfolio as of Q4 2025:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Akre Capital Management’s 13F filings for Q3 2025 and Q4 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: KCE: Not So Convinced By The AI Scare
data.description:
We cover quite a lot of capital markets players that feature in the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Capital Markets ETF (KCE), so the first thing we can comment on is the somewhat meaningful effect that these latest AI-scare trades have been having on the broader capital markets indices. The beginning of the year cemented some of the positive trends that usually emerge from lower costs of capital, namely more IPO activity usually correlated to higher market valuations and also a general return in corporate action that generates fees for financial services picks. Higher valuations have helped asset management companies too.

The KCE contains three broad sub-industries:

  • Asset management and custody banks (almost 50%)
  • Investment banking and brokerage (~30%) which is sensitive to M&A and capital markets transaction volumes and sizes, but also to countercyclical volatility in the public markets businesses
  • Financial exchanges and data providers (~20%)

that are all differently affected by the emergence of some recent LLM based AI and the overall AI-scare trade that's reared its head in markets last week. Our comment is that commodification had already come for a lot of these industries, particularly AM and exchanges, with basic automation, and we aren't sure that LLMs or AI would necessarily displace that much more revenue or steepen declines more than automation and index funds and the like already have. In other areas, we think the models are fundamentally very resilient to LLM-based AI. We aren't taking the AI threat, particularly as it has materialised in headlines now - these concerns could have been just as valid a year ago - very seriously at all. Perhaps that will prove foolish but it is our feeling on the industry.

We are therefore not negative on KCE after the sell off, though we note it's procyclical and therefore have our reservations due to our concerns about how broader markets might perform on AI-bubble fears. In particular, we are worried about growth concerns on pressure on the job market which can negatively transmit into consumer confidence and the overall economy. Therefore, we prefer countercyclical bets, which KCE is ultimately not.

KCE Breakdown

The main thing really was the aforementioned sub-industry breakdown. This is our position on the AI-scare trade with respect to each sub-industry.

  • For asset management our feeling is that equity markets had been improving and that was an important AUM and therefore profit driver. Perhaps the AI scare might affect broader market values, and this would be a threat. But in terms of the technological risk, much of asset management has already been significantly commodified. Active management has lost its lustre a long time ago, and most are quasi-indexing anyway. We don't think AI is key is accelerating this at all. If anything, LLMs may make it easier for AM firms to generate and market their quasi-indexing solutions, which we don't believe require serious insight anyway. LLMs may already provide sufficient quality for the vast majority of so-called active strategies. LLMs may also make an actually differentiated strategy more rare and could be good for the AM companies actually doing a proper job.
  • For investment banking and brokerage, increased mix of retail activity spurred by a democratisation of technology that gives confidence (perhaps not insight though) to the general public could be good for public markets brokerage. For investment banking, we think that on the supply side, banks need to keep training analysts in order to commodify senior bankers which generate most of the cost down the line after a lot of industry attrition up the ranks. On the demand side, we don't see clients replacing bankers who ultimately do provide a needed layer of due diligence and attention to detail, as well as a lot of non-public know-how and network value.
  • Financial data and exchange companies should have more value in a market with more participation from LLMs. Data is obviously valuable for training all sorts of models, and exchanges benefit much in the same way public market brokers would.

Bottom Line

Our biggest gripe with KCE is that there are very different companies within capital markets. Public brokerage is countercyclical, while IB outside of restructuring is pro-cyclical. Some businesses are human capital intensive like IB and others like exchanges are very automated in most cases. The current environment is more uncertain than usual, it would make more sense with that conviction to cut out stocks that are pro-cyclical. Specifically, the pro-cyclical sensitivity in this ETF would be the asset management businesses like for example Wisdomtree (WT) or investment banks like Perella Weinberg (PWP). Picks to keep would be brokerage stocks, like Interactive Brokers (IBKR) in terms of earnings direction, and exchange like the CME Group (CME), which are countercyclical bets in that they are pro-volatility bets typical of downmarkets. An ETF that captures this more but not the pro-cyclical elements of the KCE is the iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchanges ETF (IAI) at PE 21x, which is higher than the KCE's 14.4x for the reason that it is more recession resistant and a good hedge in a downmarket.

On the other hand there are parts of capital markets that are seeing latent growth like aspects of advisory, such as ECM, which has been doing quite well, though is low visibility and ultimately pro-cyclical. ECM deals get suddenly shelved when markets go sour.

We just aren't that keen on the concept of capital markets as a single factor which is what the KCE captures, since we don't think it's specific enough or takes sufficiently into account very diverse economics of the constituent businesses. We also do note the strong correlation with the (IYF) which is a broad financial ETF and therefore must say that KCE is ultimately more procyclical than not. More fundamentally, more than 50% of the allocation is to AM and advisory indexed to IB and M&A which are definitely procyclical businesses. That being the case, with the AI scare trade still affecting markets and reflecting a serious concern about displaced labour and services, we think that there is downside to the consumer economy in the US as job cuts surge a bit and hiring remains very mute. We would not make a pro-cycle bet in 2026 which is what KCE ultimately is, and we'd prefer to focus on the subset of the KCE that does well in a volatile market.

data.title: Palmer Square Capital BDC: Trading At A Massive Discount For A Reason
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered Palmer Square Capital BDC (PSBD), I issued a sell rating due to the weak earnings and limited portfolio growth at the time. Since my last coverage, PSBD's share price has fallen by nearly 19%, and I believe that there is still some downside risk. PSBD is set to release its Q4 earnings at the end of this month, so I wanted to revisit this business development company to reassess its overall value proposition throughout 2026. Unfortunately, I do not believe there to be any meaningful catalyst in the near term, which means that earnings may remain flat and the NAV may continue to decline.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PSBD's share price has declined by 28.1% and failed to participate in the positive market momentum over the last few quarters. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of 18.4% over the same time frame. Due to the continuous decline in share price, PSBD now offers a starting dividend yield of 14.6%. However, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of the payouts over the next twelve months.

PSBD now trades at its deepest discount to NAV pricing ever, but I do not see this as an opportunity to accumulate. I believe that the portfolio may continue to erode in the near term and the higher interest rate environment may continue to suppress its growth potential. PBSD's NAV continues to decline, and this is a clear indication of some underlying flaws that need to be addressed. Although PSBD's non-accruals remain low, it seems that management has struggled to allocate a meaningful level of capital towards new investment activity.

Financials & Earnings Estimate

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, PSBD has investments at a fair value of $1.25B that are spread across a diverse range of debt securities. Unlike traditional equities, PSBD's success does not rely on the earnings growth of its assets. Instead, PSBD essentially lends capital to a wide range of middle-market borrowers and relies on their ability to keep up with their debt maintenance. A borrower's ability to repay their loans is typically a much easier calculation for management to make, but the success can be influenced by the interest rate environment.

PSBD leans most heavily on senior secured first lien debt, which is ideal because this form of debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. So in a scenario where a borrower is struggling to keep up with debt payments and defaults, PSBD would be entitled to repayment before other forms of debt. This portfolio's breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • First Lien Debt: 86%
  • Second Lien Debt: 5%
  • Other: 5%
  • Short-Term Investments: 4%

Expanding on this diversity, PSBD also tries to maintain exposure to different industries to help reduce concentration risks. As we can see below, PSBD is most heavily allocated to software securities at 11% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to healthcare and IT services investments, which accounts for 10% and 9% of assets, respectively.

Despite the diversity, this has not helped PSBD produce strong earnings growth over the last year. For instance, PSBD previously reported its Q3 earnings, and the results were a bit mixed. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.43 per share, which was a decline from the prior year's net investment income of $0.48 per share. Additionally, total investment income landed at $31.6M, which was also a decline from the prior year's total investment income of $37.7M.

It seems like the largest driver of the lower earnings was the decline in interest income received from portfolio companies. Interest income amounted to $30.4M in the most recent quarter, which was a decline from the prior year's interest income of $35.7M. This could be attributed to the lower interest rate environment over the last year, as well as failure to offset repayments with new investment activity.

On a more positive note, PSBD was able to slightly offset the lower earnings by reducing some of its operating expenses. Total operating expense amounted to $18M in the last quarter, which was an improvement over the prior year's total operating expenses of $21.5M. Management was able to reduce their incentive fees to help offset the lower earnings. Additionally, the total interest expenses paid were also reduced down to $12.7M, which is a sign that management has been actively trying to reduce their overall debt burden.

PSBD is set to report their Q4 earnings on February 26th, and I do not have the confidence that there will be substantial improvement. The balance sheet doesn't necessarily indicate management's ability to increase earnings, since cash and equivalents only amounted to $4.2M in the last quarter, while total notes still sit around $302M.

The projected earnings estimate sits around $0.42 per share, which I believe to be reasonable. While I don't think there have been any growth catalysts in the last quarter, I also don't think there are any major red flags within its portfolio that can cause the earnings to substantially decline. Therefore, I anticipate net investment income to land somewhere between the $0.38 to $0.42 per share range for its Q4 earnings.

Valuation & Risks

When I previously covered PSBD, it traded at a discount to NAV of 11.5% at the time. Following the continuous decline in share price, PSBD now trades at a much deeper discount to NAV of 23.47%. For reference, PSBD has only traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.68% over the last three-year period. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that PSBD now trades at its deepest discount to NAV valuation since its inception. Despite this discount, I do not believe this is a good time to accumulate, as I believe there are more downside risks throughout 2026.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $12.90 per share, which indicates a potential upside of ~13.6% from the current level. However, I disagree with this outlook because I don't see any short-term catalysts that can take the price higher. Management has struggled to allocate a meaningful amount of capital towards new investment activity over the last year. For instance, PSBD was able to allocate $138.6M towards new investments last quarter. However, this was offset by $156M in sales and repayments, resulting in a net decrease of $17.3M for the quarter.

The failure to produce new investments that outpace repayments means that the NAV will continue to decline. A declining NAV is one of the clearest indications of a structural flaw within a BDC's portfolio. As the NAV declines, the underlying share price will also decline and erode an investor's capital over time. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts over the last year:

  • Q3 2024: $16.61 per share
  • Q4 2024: $16.50 per share
  • Q1 2025: $15.85 per share
  • Q2 2025: $15.68 per share
  • Q3 2025: $15.39 per share

So although PBSD's non-accruals are relatively low, this doesn't necessarily translate to an efficient portfolio strategy. Non-accruals represent the amount of portfolio companies that are struggling to keep up with their required debt maintenance. A portfolio company that is put into non-accrual status is not actively contributing to a BDC's earnings. Based on the last earnings call, management confirmed that non-accruals only amounted to 0.40% of fair value.

While credit quality is a top concern across the sector, non-accruals continue to be low at PSBD. On a fair value basis, it is only 40 basis points and on an at-cost basis, only 101 basis points. Our PIK income as a percentage of total investment income remains well below our largest peers and below the industry at approximately 1.14%. We take pride in knowing our shareholders do not have to wonder about the quality of our disclosed investment income.

- Matthew Bloomfield, President

Despite this, I believe that the higher interest rate environment is the biggest threat to PSBD's success. A higher interest rate means that the cost of debt is high, so there are fewer businesses seeking debt financing. This sort of environment limits the amount of deals available for PSBD to capitalize on, which limits growth potential. Despite the Fed initiating several interest rate cuts throughout 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range.

Tighter Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.36 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 14.6%. Alongside the base distribution, management also announced a supplemental distribution of $0.07 per share that was issued to help pay out excess income generated from its positions. As previously mentioned, net investment income amounted to $0.43 per share for the quarter. Although this indicates that earnings support the distributions being paid, I am still cautious about the overall sustainability of payouts going forward.

I would actually argue that management's decision to pay out the excess income above the distribution amount is actually hurting its ability to regain some positive NAV momentum. Instead of paying out the extra capital to investors, this cash flow can be reallocated back into growth positions that would result in positive earnings growth. Since PSBD isn't strategically using this capital to offset the flat earnings, I do not think this is a great position for investors seeking a reliable source of income.

The BDC environment can change rapidly, and even though non-accruals are low right now, this may not always be the case. Although I think it's unlikely, a scenario where interest rates rise can result in further portfolio deterioration. Or in a scenario where interest rate cuts do not happen this year, a higher rate environment may continue to put pressure on existing portfolio companies and limit returns for PSBD.

Lastly, investors need to consider the fact that distributions received from PSBD may not offer tax efficiency. Since distributions are being supported by interest income received from borrowers, the income is classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax consequences for investors. Therefore, a position in PSBD would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my sell rating on PSBD going into the BDC's Q4 earnings. I do not see any short-term catalysts that can reverse the NAV declines. The BDC is not allocating sufficient capital towards new investment activity that can improve its underlying earnings. Additionally, management has chosen to pay out earnings above the distribution amount as a supplemental distribution, which I don't believe is the best use of capital. So despite PSBD now trading at its deepest discount to NAV valuation ever, I believe the risk is not worth the high yield.

data.title: Huntington Ingalls Industries: Late To The Naval Defense Party, But I Am Upgrading
data.description:
Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII) stock prices have increased almost 150% since my last report. I had a hold rating on HII and that means that while holding the stock definitely would not have been a bad thing, overall this is a missed opportunity as the stock price performance looks more like a buy. In this report, I discuss the company’s Q4 2025 earnings, the outlook for 2026, and I update my price target and rating as aerospace and defense is entering a supercycle, which in my view justifies a buy rating along with improving throughput.

Strong Growth For Huntington Ingalls Industries In Q4 2025

HII sales grew 15.7% in Q4 2025 to $3.5 billion, exceeding analyst estimates by $377.3 million, or 12.2%. Ingalls Shipbuilding revenues rose 20.8% to $889 million, driven by higher volumes on amphibious assault ships and surface combatant programs. Newport New Shipbuilding revenues increased 19.1% to $1.9 billion driven by higher submarine and aircraft carrier volumes. Mission Technologies sales rose 2.5% to $731 million, driven by higher warfare systems and unmanned system sales.

Segment operating earnings surged 89.3%, from $103 million to $195 million, with margins increasing from 3.4% to 5.6%. Ingalls Shipbuilding profits increased almost 50% to $84 million, driven by higher volumes and lower unfavourable cost adjustments. This drove the margins from 6.3% to 7.6%. Newport News Shipbuilding profits rose from $38 million to $84 million, driven by higher volumes and favorable cost adjustments for the Virginia-class submarine compared to unfavourable adjustment on the contracts for the Virginia-class and Columbia-class submarines in the same quarter last year. Mission Technologies profits surged from $19 million to $43 million, driven by higher volume and better performance.

Why Huntington Ingalls Industries Stock Surged

I believe that the stock price of HII surged for several reasons. The first one is the throughput, which increased 14%. Previously the pressure item for HII was simply that while demand was high, the shipbuilding and submarine supply chain was heavily constrained, and that also added costs. The fourth quarter and full-year performance give a strong indication that shipbuilding and submarine programs are largely derisked and the flow is improving. Additionally, on the back of higher defense spending, the prospects for aerospace and defense companies, and especially for the more capital-intensive ship and submarine builders, have substantially improved. This provides a solid base for a rerating of the stock prices. Additionally, with interventions in Venezuela and power projections in Iran, we are seeing that aircraft carriers are not obsolete. This at some point seems to have been the administration’s stance, but we note that President Trump has been a proponent of building a stronger naval fleet. That was the case during his first term and his second term does not deviate from that in my view.

Low Growth Outlook For HII In 2026

For 2026, HII is aiming for $9.7-$9.9 billion in revenues compared to $9.6 billion in 2025. At the midpoint, this marks 2.2% growth. Margins are guided at 5.5%-6.5% compared to margins of 5.9% in 2025. Mission Technologies revenues are guided at $3.0-$3.2 billion in 2026 compared to $3.04 billion in sales for 2025. Margins are expected to be 5%, which is stable year-on-year. At the mid-point, HII is guiding for a 3.3% increase in sales and a 3.6% increase in operating profits with free cash flow of $500-$600 million compared to $800 million in 2025. So, the full-year outlook is not necessarily an impressive one and is unlikely to be a major fundamental driver to the stock price targets.

Huntington Ingalls Industries Stock Is A Buy On An Industry Rerating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The growth figures for Huntington Ingalls Industries are not necessarily impressive. Revenues are set to grow 5.3% annually with a 10.8% EBITDA growth. However, driven by a pull-forward of free cash flow into 2025, the free cash will not show strong growth. For HII, I definitely missed the huge run up. That run-up is not necessarily driven by better-than-expected results, but by a rerating as for aerospace and defense companies. The obvious risk is that if that re-rating does not hold, upside would evaporate. The upside is currently around 8%, and that doesn’t match my criteria for a buy rating as I typically look for 10% upside. However, we note that annualized upside does meet the 10% criterion. So I am marking the stock with a buy rating and a $450.72 price target, noting 23% upside against earnings expectations for 2028 and even a modest correction on the share price would offer a more compelling entry point.

Margins are expected to increase in the years ahead, driven by higher throughput driving operating leverage. Furthermore, the revenue mix will increasingly lean on post-pandemic contract awards from 2027 onward. That also means that cost overhang, such as inflationary pressures on fixed-price contracts, will slowly but surely fade bringing the company into a stronger margin position. The company’s yield is not necessarily impressive, but I do believe that the company’s substantial improvement in the net debt leverage could result in HII restarting share repurchases, which currently have not been modeled in.

Conclusion: Huntington Ingalls Stock Has Done Surprisingly Well

Looking at the stock price performance, I can only say that I adjusted the rating on the stock too early. I had not expected that the stock would see substantial tailwinds from improved sentiment. However, with global expanding defense budgets, it is undeniable that many aerospace and defense industries are seeing a rerating on their stock prices, and that drives the buy rating for Huntington Ingalls Industries, along with improving throughput, indicating that the industrial base is incrementally improving its ability to meet demand.

data.title: Micron: DDR5 Prices Surge +400% Since September And Could Rise Further In 2026
data.description:
Still Trading at 12x forward P/E

Micron Technology, Inc.'s (MU) stock has rallied +60% since my last rating. The key catalyst behind this relentless rally is the supply constraint on memory chips, which gives the company significant pricing power.

In January, they announced a $100 billion New York "megafab" site with the goal of producing 40% of its DRAM in the U.S. (DRAM represents 79% of total revenue). They also announced a $24 billion Singapore capacity expansion plan. These investments have boosted optimism that MU's growth outlook will be tremendous if it can quickly narrow the supply gap.

Assuming robust AI demand in 2026, we can see this strong growth outlook has already been priced into MU's P/E multiple, which is expected to drop from 36x (based on trailing 12 months EPS) to only 12x (based on forward 12 months EPS consensus).

In my previous analysis, despite the stock jumping 130% from the August 2025 low, I still upgraded MU to Strong Buy before its 1Q FY2026 earnings for three reasons: increasing HBM and DRAM demand, tremendous fundamental improvements (such as margins, earnings, and FCF), and 14x non-GAAP P/E fwd. Although it can be intimidating to be bullish after seeing a parabolic rally in such a short period, MU's forward P/E has become even 14% cheaper since my last rating. This suggests that its near-term earnings growth potential can surpass the recent price appreciation. If the outlook doesn't deviate from current consensus, MU is still a Strong Buy, as there is still room for further multiple expansion.

The Recent Tech Selloff Unlikely Affect MU

As AI continues to evolve, it's inevitable that some industries or companies could face severe disruptions. For example, the 21% YTD pullback in the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) reflects growing concerns that many software companies are at risk of being replaced by AI. The AI company, Anthropic, released its new AI model, showing that the AI coding agent can write and debug code, which has potential to "destroy" the traditional application software industry. Meanwhile, OpenAI announced its new version of the AI coding agent, Codex-Spark. These announcements spooked the market, triggering a broad-based tech selloff.

However, AI hardware stocks have significantly outperformed this year because better AI performance will require more powerful hardware to support faster computing and other capabilities. Therefore, as long as AI demand remains strong, I believe MU's growth will have room to improve, and the stock could continue to perform well.

Gaining Market Share

MU faces two major competitors in the global memory market: SK hynix and Samsung. Both companies' stocks have also experienced tremendous rallies over the past six months. Together, these three companies dominate the memory industry. According to the recent comments from Morgan Stanley, "DDR5 spot pricing is up another 30% YTD and currently 130% above the January contract, up 86% from December." Given the increasing supply constraint, they expect that mainstream pricing to rise further and could potentially double again this year.

Industry competition has also intensified. According to Counterpoint Research, SK hynix still holds the largest market share as a global top DRAM supplier. The company will provide its HBM products for Nvidia (NVDA)'s early Rubin volume. However, as shown on the chart above, MU is catching up. The company has gained significant DRAM market share from 21% to 26% within a 12-month period.

In addition, MU's HBM market share has doubled from 11% to 22% in a year. During the recent Wolfe Research Auto, Auto Tech and Semiconductor Conference 2026 in February, management noted that the company's strong financial outlook has been driven by a "favorable price environment". MU's HBM4 is receiving strong demand from NVDA. I believe the company has potential to secure more orders if they can ramp up HBM4 production faster.

Strong Pricing Power, Gross Margin Jump

The DRAM and HBM pricing momentums have been incredibly strong, but are they sustainable? The short answer is likely not in the long term. We would eventually see some margin normalization at some point. When supply constraints ease, prices will decrease as volumes increase. However, in the near term, more price hikes will boost the gross margin. The company previously guided 51.5% non-GAAP gross margin for 1Q FY2026. The actual number came in at 58.8%. Moreover, its 2Q FY2026 gross margin is expected to jump even higher to a new all-time high of 68%.

Let's look at earnings. MU beat its previous 1Q FY2026 EPS guidance by 27%. After three consecutive quarters of triple digit growth in non-GAAP EPS, the 1Q result suggested a sequential growth acceleration to 167% YoY. Management even guided for nearly 440% YoY growth in 2Q FY2026. This explains why the stock has experienced such a parabolic rally over the past few months.

However, there is a catch: the company's guidance and market consensus are estimated, which means that these numbers can turn out to be totally wrong if we see an unexpectedly slowdown in AI demand and exogenous shocks to the semiconductor industry, such as policy and macro risks.

How to Interpret This Cheap Valuation

MU's current valuation is a little tricky. Since September last year, the stock has rallied nearly 250%. During the same period, SK hynix and Samsung's stock jumped 220% and 75%, respectively. On a TTM basis, SK hynix's P/E is 14x and Samsung's is 27x, according to Seeking Alpha. Given the current revenue growth and margin outlook, their forward P/E ratios will be significantly lower.

As mentioned earlier, the forward P/E has dropped to 12x, even lower than 14x two months ago, despite a 60% rally. This basically tells that the stock has fully priced in the continued price squeeze on global memory chips due to supply constraints.

Based on Memory Price Trends, overall DDR5 pricing has jumped by more than 400% since September 2025. The question is whether these prices will reverse or stay elevated. MU's forward valuation multiples look very cheap because of this trend. If AI demand slows sharply, prices could drop. This would make the current earnings consensus too high. As those estimates are revised downward, MU's valuation could suddenly become much more expensive.

"Fat Tail" Risk and "Value Trap"

Based on what we've just discussed about MU's product price tailwind, let's dive a little deeper into the company's "fat tail" risk. I believe that risk could be more devastating given the magnitude of the recent rally. This is not a bubble. Like I said, if these product prices reverse significantly, MU's growth and margin will likely normalize. Trading at 12x forward P/E could explain why investors haven't pushed the multiple higher yet, because they believe this cheap valuation is unsustainable and could be a "value trap". In this context, I define a value trap not as deterioration in MU's business but as a normalization from an extreme growth momentum.

Conclusion

In summary, MU's recent rally since September has been largely driven by favorable pricing and supply constraints on DRAM and HBM. The market believes that AI demand remains robust in 2026. DDR5 prices have gone up by more than 400% within six months, and the consensus indicates prices could double again in 2026. That's why MU can maintain a triple-digit EPS growth and even will accelerate strongly in 2Q FY2026. If this trend can continue, I believe MU's valuation still looks cheap for FY2026. Given this, I reiterate Strong Buy on the stock.

However, the key downside risk is that these elevated prices may not be sustainable if AI demand unexpectedly weakens, but for now, it's still a tail risk.
